title: Mystical References in Evangelion
wikitext:
[]: Mystical References in Evangelion             Quote Post by Darth Plato » Sun Jan 04, 2026 9:08 am So, before anyone corrects me, I know that the creators disavowed any deeper connection to western esoterica or religion. While they may have been playing it down, it's clear that to anyone in Japan the whole thing is just a fictional mythos. I admit I tried to find more depth to it at my first exposure and that this had to be explained to me. Basically to 99% of the people in Japan, western religious references are just exotic and it can be taken superficially. That said there are a number of younger people on the forum and even people who are older may not have the breadth of interests I do in such things, so many probably don't have the background I could bring to discussion. So I am offering it here. We'll start with the Dead Sea Scrolls. It might be helpful for viewers to know that although these discoveries began in 1947, access to the manuscripts or even facsimiles of them was highly restricted for a seriously long time. In fact the whole restored corpus was not published complete until 1991. This made headlines around the world because it was strange that the scrolls were kept under lock and key so to speak which is not normal with manuscript discoveries, especially ones that important. So I think the fact that the PUBLICATION of the scrolls was so recent made it a fresh and interesting thing to include DSS in the story. In particular, NGE purports that there were OTHER scrolls that were NEVER published because SEELE got its hands on them, and that these secret Dead Sea Scrolls included information concerning the end of the world and the sequence of Angels who would attack. Apparently in the NGE mythos, the prophecies can play out in multiple ways and SEELE wants to manipulate that. The Dead Sea Scrolls DO include apocalyptic material (I've read them, since I study ancient literature pretty heavily) and one discovered text in there describes a figure called Armilus who will lead an unholy army at the end of the age. There is an alternate continuity of novels that actually has a figure called Armilus although I have not read it. But anyway, Armilus is considered by scholars to be the Jewish prophecy concept that was later identified by Christians as the Antichrist--an evil leader who would hold power at the very end of the age before God ends the world and brings humanity to judgment. Now, apart from that alternate continuity it occurs to me that perhaps Gendo could be considered Armilus or the Antichrist. In support of this he created a "666 field" to protect Nerv, and of course this number is associated with the idea of the Antichrist. Additionally, it is said that the Antichrist will claim to be or attempt to become God. To be clear, I am considering these things as literature here. I am not attempting to start a religious or theological discussion. I am discussing it the way one discusses literature or comic books or anime, so hopefully no one will be offended. So, what do you think--is Gendo the Antichrist? I don't think this notion is too complicated for it to have been part of the "general" intention of the creators. For literary purposes of course--not religious ones." My second question if people follow this thread (and there can be more on this topic) is how do you think the DSS are intended to contain useful information in the story? Are human religions supposed to be somehow connected with the influence of the Angels and the primordial species that seeded them on the earth? Are we just not supposed to think about it?

[]: Re: Mystical References in Evangelion             Quote Post by ChaddyManPrime » Mon Jan 19, 2026 5:56 pm I personally like the idea that Shinji is the Anti-Christ

[]: Re: Mystical References in Evangelion             Quote Post by asakuraikun » Mon Jan 26, 2026 11:19 pm Well, in a literary sense I think it just shows that many cultures and traditions have a similar structure about the end of times. I don't know in which form these first reached Japan (the closest geographically may have been Hinduism with Kali Yuga, but later with christians too), so it would be quite unsurprising if it appears in this form in Japanese writing too, including anime eventually. Usually the anti-christ narrative, and other similar traditions usually follow more or less this structure: 1. A crisis exists. This can be damning event, or times who are seen by a tradition as morally corrupt. 2. A deceiving figure appears, who at first seems to be a saviour in difficult times. 3. The deceiving figure usually does not reject the sacred entity established before, and actually uses it to gain trust. 4. There's usually a relatively stable time after this. 5. When this period expires, people allow the deceiving figure to use any means to return to it. That includes violence, violation of sacred values and so on. 6. In most cases, here is where the actual messiah appears. He wins by not using the methods of the anti-christ, and starts an era of restoration. Again, just like you, I mean the structure and not the events themselves, because it is different depending on the religion, but they are quite similar in this structure. I can see some bits of Evangelion here and there, so I don't think it is an impossible association EVEN IF the author did not actually intend to do so, simply cause we see this in various places, including media.
